chapter_summary: The Mandukya Upanishad begins with an invocation and then introduces Om as the ultimate reality encompassing all time and the timeless. It declares that the Self (Atman) is Brahman and has four quarters (padas): Vaisvanara (waking state), Taijasa (dream state), Prajna (deep sleep), and Turiya (the fourth, transcendent). The first pada, Vaisvanara, is described in detail as the waking state with seven limbs and nineteen mouths, enjoying gross objects.

story.title: Mandukya Upanishad: The Four Quarters of the Self
story.arc: Exposition of the nature of Om and the four states of consciousness leading to the realization of Turiya.
story.timeline_note: Timeless; the teaching is presented as a direct revelation.
story.comic_potential: Low; it is a philosophical exposition with no narrative action.

teachings.teaching: Om is the entire universe, past, present, future, and beyond time.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, Om is the primordial vibration that underlies all creation; meditating on Om leads to the dissolution of duality.

teachings.teaching: The Self (Atman) is Brahman and has four quarters.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that the four states correspond to levels of consciousness accessible through Kriya-Yoga practices, culminating in Turiya, the witness state.

teachings.teaching: Vaisvanara is the waking state, with seven limbs and nineteen mouths, enjoying gross objects.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma interprets the seven limbs as the subtle energy centers (chakras) and the nineteen mouths as the senses and inner faculties; Kriya-Yoga purifies these to transcend the waking state.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Mandukya Upanishad with Gaudapada's Karika describes the three states of consciousness (jagrat, svapna, suṣupti) and their corresponding aspects of the Self: Vaiśvānara (Viśva), Taijasa, and Prājña. It explains the characteristics of each state, the locations in the body, and culminates in the fourth state (turīya) which is non-dual. Gaudapada's verses reinforce the unity underlying the three states.

story.title: The Three States of Consciousness and the Fourth
story.arc: Exposition of the nature of the Self through the analysis of waking, dream, and deep sleep states.

teachings.teaching: The Self has four quarters: Vaiśvānara (waking), Taijasa (dream), Prājña (deep sleep), and the fourth (turīya) which is non-dual.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this maps to the three states of consciousness experienced in meditation: waking awareness, subtle dream-like visions, and the state of deep absorption (samadhi) leading to the realization of the non-dual Self.

teachings.teaching: Vaiśvānara experiences gross objects through the senses; Taijasa experiences subtle objects in the mind; Prājña is a mass of consciousness, blissful, and the gateway to other states.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that the yogi must transcend the gross (sthūla) and subtle (sūkṣma) to reach the causal (kāraṇa) state, which corresponds to Prājña, and then go beyond to turīya.

teachings.teaching: Prājña is the lord of all, omniscient, inner controller, and the source of all beings.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga view that the causal state (kāraṇa śarīra) is the seat of the individual self (jīva) as Īśvara, and through meditation one realizes this as the universal Self.

teachings.teaching: The three states are one and the same Self, like a great fish moving in different waters.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma uses this analogy to illustrate that the same consciousness appears different due to upādhis (limiting adjuncts), and Kriya-Yoga aims to remove these adjuncts through prāṇāyāma and meditation.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Mandukya Upanishad with Gaudapada's Karika discusses the three states of consciousness (jagrat, svapna, susupti) and their corresponding entities (Visva, Taijasa, Prajna), the nature of creation as illusion (maya), and the ultimate reality of Turiya beyond all states. It emphasizes non-duality and the identity of the individual self with Brahman.

story.title: The Three States and the Fourth
story.arc: Exposition of the three states of consciousness and their unity in Turiya

teachings.teaching: The three states (jagrat, svapna, susupti) are experienced by Visva, Taijasa, and Prajna respectively, but they are all one consciousness.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this corresponds to the integration of waking, dream, and deep sleep states through meditative awareness, realizing the underlying unity of consciousness.

teachings.teaching: One who knows the enjoyer and the enjoyed as one is not tainted by enjoyment.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that through Kriya-Yoga, the practitioner realizes the identity of the subject and object, leading to liberation from karma.

teachings.teaching: All beings are born from Prana (the seed) and are manifestations of consciousness.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga view that prana is the creative force, and by controlling prana, one can realize the unity of all existence.

teachings.teaching: Creation is like a dream or magic; it is not ultimately real.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's commentary would stress that the world is a projection of the mind, and through Kriya-Yoga, one can transcend this illusion.

teachings.teaching: Some say creation is by will, others by time; but these are mere speculations.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would likely dismiss such debates as irrelevant to the direct experience of the Self through Kriya-Yoga.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Mandukya Upanishad with Gaudapada's Karika discusses the nature of the fourth state (turīya) beyond the three states of waking, dreaming, and deep sleep. It refutes various theories of creation (from will, time, enjoyment, or play) and establishes turīya as the non-dual, indescribable, and peaceful Self. The commentary emphasizes that turīya is not a separate entity but the true nature of the Self realized through negation of the three states, akin to recognizing the rope after the snake illusion is dispelled.

story.title: The Fourth State: Turīya
story.arc: Philosophical exposition on the nature of the Self and the fourth state beyond the three states of consciousness.
story.timeline_note: Non-linear; the chapter is a commentary on the Mandukya Upanishad, presenting a timeless philosophical argument.
story.comic_potential: Low; the content is abstract and philosophical, with no narrative or dramatic elements.

teachings.teaching: The fourth state (turīya) is not conscious of inner, outer, or both; it is not a mass of consciousness, not conscious, nor unconscious. It is unseen, beyond empirical dealings, ungraspable, without characteristics, unthinkable, indescribable, and essentially the Self.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would interpret this as the state of pure awareness in Kriya-Yoga, where the practitioner transcends all mental modifications and abides in the Self.

teachings.teaching: Turīya is the lord of all, the controller of the cessation of all sufferings, non-dual, and all-pervading.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this corresponds to the realization of the Self as the witness and master of all experiences, leading to liberation from suffering.

teachings.teaching: Viśva and taijasa are bound by cause and effect, prājña is bound by cause alone, but turīya is free from both.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would see this as a map of consciousness: the waking and dream states are tied to karma, deep sleep to ignorance, but the fourth state is absolute freedom.

teachings.teaching: Prājña does not know the Self, others, truth, or falsehood; turīya is the eternal seer of all.
teachings.lens_note: This highlights that even the bliss of deep sleep is not the final realization; only turīya, the witness, knows all without duality.

chapter_summary: This chapter from Gaudapada's Karika on the Mandukya Upanishad discusses the nature of the four states of consciousness (jagrat, svapna, susupti, and turiya) and the process of awakening to non-dual reality. It explains that the dualistic world is mere illusion (maya) and that the turiya state is beyond sleep and dream, characterized by non-duality. The chapter emphasizes that through the grace of a guru and understanding of the teachings, one realizes the unborn, sleepless, dreamless, non-dual Self.

story.title: Awakening to the Fourth State
story.arc: The chapter presents a philosophical discourse on the four states of consciousness and the path to realizing non-duality. It begins by distinguishing the states, then explains the role of sleep and dream in bondage, and culminates in the teaching that through the guru's instruction, one awakens to the unborn, non-dual Self.
story.timeline_note: Timeless; the teaching is about the eternal nature of consciousness.
story.comic_potential: The chapter is abstract and philosophical, with no narrative action. Comic potential is low.

teachings.teaching: The dualistic world is mere illusion (māyā); non-duality is the ultimate truth.
teachings.lens_note: In Shailendra Sharma's Kriya-Yoga commentary, this teaching emphasizes that the perception of duality arises from ignorance (avidyā) and is dissolved through direct realization of the Self, which is the goal of Kriya-Yoga practice.

teachings.teaching: The turīya state is beyond sleep and dream, characterized by non-duality and constant awareness.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga interprets turīya as the state of sahaja samadhi, where the practitioner abides in the natural, effortless awareness of the Self, free from the modifications of mind.

teachings.teaching: Awakening occurs through the grace of a guru who reveals the truth 'That thou art' (tat tvam asi).
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, the guru is essential for transmitting the technique of pranayama and meditation that leads to the direct experience of the Self, as described in this verse.

teachings.teaching: The distinctions of teacher, teaching, and student are also illusory and dissolve in non-dual realization.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's commentary would note that in the highest state, even the Kriya-Yoga techniques and the guru-disciple relationship are transcended, revealing the unity of all.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Mandukya Upanishad with Gaudapada Karika explains the identity of the four states of consciousness (Vaishvanara, Taijasa, Prajna) with the three matras (A, U, M) of Omkara, and the benefits of knowing this identity. It also includes a karika summarizing the correspondence.

story.title: Identity of Omkara and the Three States of Consciousness
story.arc: Exposition of the correspondence between the matras of Om and the states of consciousness, with benefits of knowledge.
story.timeline_note: Part of the Mandukya Upanishad and Gaudapada Karika, likely composed around 6th century CE.

teachings.teaching: The first matra A corresponds to Vaishvanara (waking state); knowing this grants all desires and becomes first.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes the importance of the waking state as the foundation for spiritual practice, where one must first establish awareness (A) to progress.

teachings.teaching: The second matra U corresponds to Taijasa (dream state); knowing this elevates knowledge and makes one equal, and no non-Brahman-knower is born in his family.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would interpret this as the intermediate stage of practice where one transcends the waking state and enters deeper states of consciousness (U), leading to balance and spiritual lineage.

teachings.teaching: The third matra M corresponds to Prajna (deep sleep); knowing this measures all and becomes the end.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this represents the culmination where one merges into the causal state (M), realizing the unity of all and becoming the source.

teachings.teaching: When wishing to express the 'A-ness' of Vishva, the commonality of being first is prominent; when there is non-apprehension of the matras, the commonality of pervasion is also there.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma might see this as a meta-instruction: the correspondence is not merely intellectual but experiential; when one fails to grasp the matras individually, the overarching unity (pervasion) becomes the key.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Mandukya Upanishad with Gaudapada's Karika explains the correspondence between the three states of consciousness (viśva, taijasa, prājña) and the three mātrās of Oṃ (A, U, M), culminating in the fourth, amātra, which is the turīya, the non-dual, peaceful, and unutterable Self. It emphasizes that by meditating on Oṃ with understanding of its parts, one attains the highest Brahman and becomes free from fear.

story.title: The Correspondence of Oṃ and the Self
story.arc: Exposition of the identity of Oṃkāra with the four states of consciousness and the attainment of liberation through meditation.

teachings.teaching: The three mātrās of Oṃ (A, U, M) correspond to the three states of consciousness: viśva, taijasa, and prājña. By understanding this correspondence, one realizes the identity of Oṃ and the Self.
teachings.lens_note: In Shailendra Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this teaching emphasizes the practice of pranava (Oṃ) meditation as a means to integrate the waking, dream, and deep sleep states into the fourth state of turīya, which is the goal of yoga.

teachings.teaching: The fourth, amātra, is the unutterable, peaceful, non-dual turīya. One who knows this enters the Self and is not born again.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga interprets this as the culmination of kriya practice where the meditator transcends all mental modifications and abides in the pure consciousness, the seedless samadhi.

teachings.teaching: One should fix the mind on Praṇava, which is the fearless Brahman. For one ever united with Praṇava, there is no fear anywhere.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with Kriya-Yoga's emphasis on constant remembrance of the divine name (japa) and meditation on the subtle sound, leading to fearlessness and liberation.
